title: Practice like a golfer, not a ball-beater
microFact: Shots inside 100m are ~60% of your score. Spend your practice where your strokes actually live.
lesson:
The driving-range trap: buy 100 balls, hit 80 drivers, go home. It feels like practice; it's actually rehearsal of one club you use 14 times a round.

Where a 110-shooter's strokes really go: ~36+ putts, a dozen chips/pitches, several duffed short shots — roughly 60% of your total inside 100 meters. Allocate practice to match:

• 40% SHORT GAME: the byte-46 chip drill, distance-control pitches to three targets, bunker basics once you have the 56°.
• 25% PUTTING: 1m circle drill (hole 20 in a row — gimmes become real), long-putt ladder for two-putt range control.
• 35% FULL SWING — with structure: every ball gets a target and the full pre-shot routine. Play 'imaginary Clover Greens': driver, then 7-iron, then wedge, changing clubs like a real round. Random practice transfers to the course; block-hammering one club doesn't.

Your weekly rhythm on non-course days: one range session run exactly like this beats three mindless bucket-beatings. And putting practice needs no booking, no tee time, and a ₹500 mat at home covers monsoon Mondays.

quiz.question: Roughly what share of a high-handicapper's strokes happen within 100m of the hole?
quiz.explanation: Putts alone are ~35%; add chips, pitches and bunker shots and it's ~60%. Practice allocation should look like your scorecard.
